comments.text: A recent preprint by Christian Táfula, "Representation functions with prescribed rates of growth" (arXiv:2405.01530, last revised May 2026), provides new insights right at the logarithmic boundary: 1. Above the boundary: The paper proves that if $F(n)$ is a regularly varying function satisfying $\lim_{n \to \infty} F(n)/\log n = \infty$, then there exists a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that $1_A * 1_A(n) \sim F(n)$. This establishes that any rate of growth strictly faster than $\log n$ is attainable. 2. The logarithmic sparsity wall: The paper presents a probabilistic heuristic indicating that if $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ satisfies $$\limsup_{n\to\infty} \frac{1_A * 1_A(n)}{\log n} < 1,$$ then $1_A * 1_A(n) = 0$ for infinitely many $n$. Since a limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1_A * 1_A(n))/\log n = c > 0$ requires the representation function to be positive for all sufficiently large $n$, this heuristic strongly implies that no such limit can exist for any constant $c < 1$.

The best progress on this problem seems to be by (G. Horváth, "An improvement of a theorem of Erdős and Sárközy", Pollack Periodica 2 (2007, Supp. 1), 155–164) which shows that $|1_A*1_A(n)-\log(n)|\leq (1-\epsilon)\sqrt{\log n})$ cannot hold simultaneously for all sufficiently large $n$. Horváth also shows the same result for all higher self- convolutions $1_A*1_A*\dots*1_A$. (The reference was located by GPT-5.) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
